region_of_origin: Mexico (Sonora state, popularised in the United States)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated chimichanga class. The closest defensible parent is the burrito food product class (FOODON_00002809), as a chimichanga is a deep-fried or baked burrito. Vegetarian variant is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a chimichanga-specific code. A0FKR (Burrito) is the closest defensible parent category. Vegetarian and fried distinctions are not separately coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for chimichanga or vegetarian chimichanga as of the knowledge cutoff.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (bell peppers, onions, corn, zucchini)

serving.natural_language_portion: One chimichanga weighing approximately 220 g, typically served with sour cream, guacamole, or salsa on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Chimichanga, bean and cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 21340)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for bean-and-cheese chimichanga used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values are adapted to reflect a vegetarian cheese-and-vegetable filling variant; the macronutrient profile is closely comparable. Meat is absent in both; cheese and bean content is similar. Minor adjustments to fibre and sodium are noted in the record notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian chimichanga in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is estimated by analogy with wheat-flour tortilla-based mixed dishes (GI ~50–65) and bean-containing Mexican foods (refried beans GI ~38, flour tortilla GI ~30–52 per serving context). A composite mid-range estimate of 58 (medium category) is applied, consistent with the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for comparable flour-tortilla and legume-containing dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 66.9) / 100 ≈ 38.8, rounded to 38.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1056 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit vegetarian chimichanga consumption due to its high sodium content from cheese, refried beans, and seasoning; a single serving may contribute more than 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high glycemic load per serving (~38.5) from refined wheat flour tortilla and starchy bean filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume vegetarian chimichangas in moderation; the refined flour tortilla contributes a medium GI and a high glycemic load per serving, which can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise, and portion control or substitution with a wholegrain tortilla is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese (~4.5 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, vegetarian chimichangas present a moderate concern due to saturated fat from cheese and high sodium from seasoning and refried beans; choosing a baked rather than deep-fried preparation and reducing cheese quantity can meaningfully lower saturated fat and caloric density.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A vegetarian chimichanga is a Mexican-origin dish consisting of a large flour tortilla filled with a mixture of refried beans, cheese (typically cheddar or Monterey Jack), and mixed vegetables such as bell peppers, onions, corn, and zucchini, seasoned with cumin, chilli powder, and garlic. The filled tortilla is folded into a rectangular parcel and then deep-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crispy, while the interior remains soft, creamy, and savoury. A baked variant exists and is lower in fat. The chimichanga is believed to have originated in the Sonora state of Mexico and was popularised in the southwestern United States, particularly in Arizona. It is closely related to the burrito but is distinguished by its fried preparation. Nutritionally, a vegetarian chimichanga provides approximately 256 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (8.2 g), significant carbohydrate (30.4 g) from the flour tortilla and beans, and fat (11.8 g) primarily from frying oil and cheese. It is a source of dietary fibre (3.2 g per 100 g) from beans and vegetables, and provides calcium from cheese. Sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 58) and high glycemic load per serving make it a food requiring portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, vegetarian chimichangas are available at select Mexican restaurants and are typically served with sour cream, guacamole, pico de gallo, or salsa. The dish is suitable for lacto-vegetarians but not vegans due to the cheese content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a vegetarian chimichanga?
What is the difference between a chimichanga and a burrito?
How much sodium is in a chimichanga?
What is inside a veggie chimichanga?
Where to get vegetarian chimichanga in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for vegetarian chimichanga. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy bean-and-cheese chimichanga entry (NDB No. 21340), which is the closest approved-source match. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed GI tables for comparable flour-tortilla and legume-based dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adapted source is structurally very similar (same tortilla base, same bean-and-cheese filling, no meat in either).
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 21340 (bean and cheese chimichanga). The vegetarian variant omits meat and adds mixed vegetables; the macronutrient profile is closely comparable. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category. 3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are reported as -1 only where the USDA SR Legacy source does not provide a value for this adapted entry; all other -1 fields are documented here as not reported in the source database. 4. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for vegetarian chimichanga was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 5. This food is served at two field survey data restaurants (Fiesta Mexico - Three Amigos Indian and Mexican Restaurant; Three Amigos Mexican and Indian - Boat Quay) at SGD 25.00. 6. The cuisine tag 'Naan' in the input is inconsistent with a Mexican dish and has been disregarded. 7. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this Mexican-cuisine dish; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese mandatory language rules apply. The romanized Spanish form 'chimichanga vegetariana' is provided as a romanized alias.
